1:
"Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them." The brief verse notes the completion of the heavens and earth and all their host. The grammatical voices note the finished creation. Ellicott and Gill note that "the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them" — the work of creation, the heavens and earth and all that fills them, is completed; "all the host of them" embraces the sun, moon, and stars (the host of heaven) and the creatures of the earth; the whole creation, in all its fullness, is finished.

On "the heavens and the earth were finished," the reformed Calvin notes the perfection. Calvin and Gill note that the creation was "finished" — brought to its completion and perfection; nothing was lacking or unfinished; God's six-day work resulted in a complete, ordered, and furnished world; the creation is whole and perfect as God made it.

On "all the host of them," the puritan Henry notes the fullness: "all the host of them" — the numerous and varied works of creation, marshaled like an army in their order and array; the heavens with their host (the stars), the earth with its creatures; the orderly, complete furnishing of the world; all things in their ranks and stations. The verse displays the completion of the heavens and earth and all their host.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the completion — "the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them," the whole creation completed and furnished (Ellicott, Gill); the creation "finished," brought to perfection, nothing lacking (Calvin, Gill); "all the host of them," the orderly fullness of the heavens and earth (Henry). The verse displays the finished creation. The verse marks the heavens: finished. The verse displays the earth: completed. The verse displays the host: all of them. The verse displays the order: complete. The completion displays the whole creation finished and furnished. The verse displays the creation: whole. The verse displays the host: marshaled. The verse displays the work: perfect. The completion of the heavens and earth and all their host displays the close of the creative work: thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all their host — the whole creation, in all its ordered fullness, brought to completion and perfection by God. The verse displays the finished creation, into which God's rest follows (v.2).
2:
"And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made." The witnesses note God ending his work and resting on the seventh day. On "on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made," the grammatical voices note the completion. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "on the seventh day God ended his work" — God brought his creative work to its close; the work was finished by the seventh day (the sense being that by the seventh day God had completed it, and on it he ceased); the six days of creating end, and the seventh is the day of completion and rest.

On "he rested on the seventh day from all his work," the reformed Calvin notes the divine rest. Calvin and Gill note that "he rested (shabath, ceased) on the seventh day from all his work" — God's resting is not from weariness (the eternal God does not tire, Isaiah 40:28), but the ceasing from the work of creation; he stopped creating new things, the work being complete; the rest is the cessation of the creative labor.

On "from all his work which he had made," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the ceasing: God rested "from all his work which he had made" — he ceased from the work of the six days; not that he ceased to uphold and govern (his providence continues, John 5:17), but that the work of original creation was finished; God's rest sets the pattern for man's sabbath rest. The verse displays God ending his work and resting on the seventh day.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the completion and rest — "on the seventh day God ended his work... and he rested on the seventh day," God completing his work and ceasing on the seventh day (Keil, Ellicott); "he rested," the ceasing from creative labor, not from weariness (Calvin, Gill); "from all his work which he had made," the work of creation finished, though providence continues (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays God's rest. The verse marks the work: ended. The verse displays the rest: on the seventh day. The verse displays the ceasing: from his work. The verse displays the pattern: for the sabbath. The act displays God completing and resting from creation. The verse displays the work: finished. The verse displays the rest: divine. The verse displays the seventh day: hallowed. God ending his work and resting on the seventh day display the close of creation: on the seventh day God ended his work and rested from all his work — the creative labor complete, God ceasing on the seventh day, the pattern of the sabbath rest. The verse displays God's rest, on which he blesses and sanctifies the day (v.3).
3:
"And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work which God created and made." The witnesses note the blessing and sanctifying of the seventh day — the crux of the Sabbath's institution. On "God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it," the grammatical voices note the hallowing. Ellicott and Gill note that "God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it (set it apart as holy)" — God blesses and consecrates the seventh day, distinguishing it from the others; the day is hallowed because God rested on it; the sanctifying marks it as a holy day, set apart for rest and the worship of God.

On the institution of the Sabbath, interpreters divide. The reformed Calvin and Gill hold that the Sabbath was instituted and sanctified at creation, from the beginning, for all mankind — the seventh-day rest set apart by God at the world's foundation, a creation ordinance binding on the whole human race, not merely on Israel. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the alternative view: that the religious observance of the Sabbath did not begin until the days of Moses (Sinai), and that the blessing and sanctifying here are recorded proleptically (anticipating the later institution given to Israel); the formal sabbath-law belonging to the Mosaic covenant.

On "because that in it he had rested," the grammatical-historical note the ground: the day is sanctified "because in it he had rested from all his work" — God's own rest is the ground and pattern of the sabbath; the day commemorates the completion of creation; man is to rest as God rested. The verse displays the blessing and sanctifying of the seventh day.

The witnesses agree the substance: God blessed and sanctified the seventh day because he rested on it, setting it apart as holy (Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke). But the timing of the Sabbath's institution bears a crux: instituted at creation for all mankind, a creation ordinance (Calvin, Gill) vs the formal observance beginning only with Moses at Sinai for Israel, the creation-blessing recorded proleptically (Clarke notes this view). The verse displays the Sabbath crux. The verse marks the day: blessed and sanctified. The verse displays the debate: creation or Sinai. The verse displays the ground: God's rest. The verse displays the holiness: set apart. The hallowing displays the seventh day consecrated by God. The verse displays the day: holy. The verse displays the rest: commemorated. The verse displays the institution: debated. The blessing and sanctifying of the seventh day display the hallowing of the Sabbath and a crux: God blessed and sanctified the seventh day because he rested on it — read by some as the institution of the Sabbath at creation for all mankind (Calvin, Gill), and by others as anticipating the Mosaic sabbath given later to Israel (Clarke). The verse displays the hallowed day, closing the creation account (v.4).
4:
"These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens," The witnesses note the heading of the generations and the divine name LORD God. On "These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created," the grammatical voices note the heading. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "These are the generations (toledoth) of the heavens and of the earth" — the formula "these are the generations" (recurring through Genesis) introduces a new section; here it heads the account that follows (the fuller account of man and the garden, 2:4-25); the "generations" denote the history, origin, and developments of the heavens and earth.

On "when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens," the witnesses note the recapitulation. Ellicott and Gill note that the verse looks back to the creation (ch. 1) and forward to the account of man; "in the day" is used broadly for the time of creation; the second account (2:4ff.) does not contradict the first but focuses on man and his garden, complementing the cosmic account of ch. 1.

On "the LORD God," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the divine name: here for the first time the compound name "LORD God" (Yahweh Elohim) appears — joining the covenant name "LORD" (Yahweh) with "God" (Elohim); the same God who created (Elohim, ch. 1) is the covenant LORD (Yahweh) who deals with man; the name marks God as both Creator and the personal, covenant God; (some critics see in the change of names different sources, but the conservative reading takes the names as marking the different aspects of the one God's work). The verse displays the heading of the generations and the divine name LORD God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heading — "These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth," the toledoth-formula introducing the account of man and the garden (Keil, Ellicott); the verse recapitulating the creation and turning to man, the second account complementing the first (Ellicott, Gill); "the LORD God" the compound name joining the Creator (Elohim) and the covenant LORD (Yahweh), the one God in both aspects (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the generations-heading. The verse marks the heading: the generations. The verse displays the account: of heaven and earth. The verse displays the name: LORD God. The verse displays the God: Creator and covenant Lord. The heading displays the turn to the history of man under the covenant God. The verse displays the generations: introduced. The verse displays the LORD: Yahweh. The verse displays the God: Elohim. The heading of the generations and the divine name LORD God display the opening of the second creation account: these are the generations of the heavens and earth, made by the LORD God — the toledoth-heading turning to the history of man, and the compound name marking God as both Creator and covenant Lord. The verse displays the LORD God, who forms man (vv.5-7).
5:
"And every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew: for the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground." The witnesses note the earth before cultivation, without rain or tiller. On "every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew," the grammatical voices note the state before man. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew" — the account describes the state of the earth before the cultivated plants grew; the "plant of the field" and "herb of the field" are the cultivated vegetation (the plants of agriculture), which awaited rain and human tillage; the wild vegetation (ch. 1) was already made, but the cultivated growth awaited the conditions of v.5-6.

On "the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth," the witnesses note the absence of rain. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth" — there was as yet no rain; the earth was watered by a mist (v.6); the ordinary means of cultivation (rain) were not yet operating.

On "there was not a man to till the ground," the grammatical-historical note the absence of the tiller: "there was not a man to till the ground" — there was no man to cultivate the soil; the cultivated plants awaited both rain and the labor of man; the verse prepares for the forming of man (v.7) to till the garden; the earth awaited its keeper. The verse displays the earth before cultivation, without rain or tiller.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the state — "every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew," the cultivated vegetation awaiting the conditions of growth (Keil, Ellicott); "the LORD God had not caused it to rain," no rain yet, the earth watered by mist (v.6, Ellicott, Gill); "there was not a man to till the ground," no tiller yet, the earth awaiting man (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the untilled earth. The verse marks the plants: not yet grown. The verse displays the rain: not yet sent. The verse displays the man: not yet made. The verse displays the ground: untilled. The state displays the earth awaiting rain and man. The verse displays the field: barren. The verse displays the rain: absent. The verse displays the tiller: lacking. The earth before cultivation, without rain or tiller, displays the state before man's making: the cultivated plants had not yet grown, for God had not sent rain and there was no man to till the ground — the earth awaiting both rain and the labor of man. The verse displays the untilled earth, watered by a mist (v.6).
6:
"But there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground." The brief verse notes the mist watering the ground. The grammatical voices note the watering. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground" — before the rain, a mist (or vapor) rose from the earth and watered the whole surface of the ground; the mist supplied the moisture needed for the vegetation; God provided for the earth's watering by this means before the ordinary rains began.

On "there went up a mist from the earth," the witnesses note the vapor. Ellicott and Gill note that the "mist" (or rising vapor) was the means God used to water the ground in the original state; whether a daily mist or the primeval watering, it sufficed to nourish the plants; the divine provision for the earth's moisture.

On "watered the whole face of the ground," the grammatical-historical note the provision: the mist "watered the whole face of the ground" — the whole surface was moistened and made fruitful; God's provision sustained the vegetation before rain; the earth was prepared and watered for life and growth; the divine care for the creation's nourishment. The verse displays the mist watering the ground.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the watering — "there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground," a mist rising to water the ground before the rain (Keil, Ellicott); "there went up a mist," the vapor God used to water the earth (Ellicott, Gill); "watered the whole face of the ground," the whole surface moistened and made fruitful (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the watered ground. The verse marks the mist: rising. The verse displays the source: from the earth. The verse displays the watering: the whole ground. The verse displays the provision: God's. The act displays the earth watered by the rising mist. The verse displays the mist: ascending. The verse displays the ground: watered. The verse displays the earth: nourished. The mist watering the ground displays the provision for the earth before rain: a mist went up from the earth and watered the whole face of the ground — God's provision moistening and nourishing the earth before the ordinary rains. The verse displays the watered ground, from which man is formed (v.7).
7:
"And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul." The witnesses note the forming of man from dust and the breath of life. On "the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground," the grammatical voices note the forming. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD God formed (yatsar, as a potter shapes clay) man of the dust of the ground" — God shaped man's body from the dust of the earth; the word "formed" pictures the divine artistry, fashioning man's frame from the ground (adamah, whence "Adam"); man's body is of the earth, earthy, made of the dust.

On "breathed into his nostrils the breath of life," the reformed Calvin notes the soul. Calvin and Gill note that God "breathed into his nostrils the breath of life" — God imparted to the formed body the breath of life, the living, rational soul; the soul is not of the dust but the immediate gift of God's inbreathing; man's higher nature, the spirit, is from God; the breath of life makes the dust-formed body a living person.

On "man became a living soul," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the result: "man became a living soul (nephesh chayyah)" — by the union of the dust-formed body and the divine breath, man became a living being, a living soul; man is a compound of earthly body and God-given life; the dignity of man, body from the dust and soul from God's breath; the creature uniquely formed and inspired by God. The verse displays the forming of man from dust and the breath of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the forming — "the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground," God shaping man's body from the dust (Ellicott, Gill); "breathed into his nostrils the breath of life," God imparting the living, rational soul, the soul from God (Calvin, Gill); "man became a living soul," man a living being, body from dust and soul from God (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the formed man. The verse marks the body: of the dust. The verse displays the breath: of life. The verse displays the soul: from God. The verse displays the man: a living soul. The act displays man formed of dust and inspired with God's breath. The verse displays the dust: shaped. The verse displays the breath: imparted. The verse displays the man: living. The forming of man from dust and the breath of life display the making of man: the LORD God formed man of the dust and breathed into him the breath of life, and man became a living soul — man's body of the earth, his soul the immediate gift of God's inbreathing, the creature uniquely formed and inspired by God. The verse displays the living man, placed in Eden (v.8).
8:
"And the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed." The witnesses note the planting of the garden of Eden and the placing of man. On "the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden," the grammatical voices note the garden. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden" — God planted a garden (a park, paradise) in the region of Eden, to the east; Eden ("delight") is the place, the garden a special enclosure within it; God himself prepared this delightful, fruitful garden as man's first dwelling.

On "a garden eastward in Eden," the witnesses note the place. Ellicott and Gill note that the garden was in "Eden," a real region (located by the rivers, vv.10-14), to the east; the garden was a place of beauty, fruitfulness, and delight, planted by God for man; the paradise prepared for the first man.

On "there he put the man whom he had formed," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the placing: "there he put the man whom he had formed" — God placed man in the garden; man, formed of the dust (v.7), is set in this prepared, delightful dwelling; the garden is man's home, the place of his innocence, fellowship with God, and appointed work (v.15); God's care provides man a fit and happy habitation. The verse displays the planting of the garden of Eden and the placing of man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the garden — "the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden," God planting the garden/paradise in Eden, to the east (Keil, Ellicott); the garden in the real region of Eden, a place of beauty and fruitfulness (Ellicott, Gill); "there he put the man whom he had formed," God placing man in the prepared garden, his happy home (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Eden. The verse marks the garden: planted. The verse displays the place: Eden, eastward. The verse displays the man: placed there. The verse displays the dwelling: prepared. The act displays God preparing the garden and placing man in it. The verse displays the garden: of Eden. The verse displays the man: settled. The verse displays the home: delightful. The planting of the garden of Eden and the placing of man display God's provision of a home: the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden and put the man there — the delightful, fruitful paradise prepared by God as man's first dwelling and the place of his fellowship with God. The verse displays the garden, with its trees and the tree of life (v.9).
9:
"And out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil." The witnesses note the trees of the garden, the tree of life, and the tree of knowledge. On "every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food," the grammatical voices note the garden's trees. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food" — God made the garden's trees both beautiful (pleasant to the sight) and fruitful (good for food); the garden provided for man's delight and sustenance; the trees combined beauty and use, the bounty of God.

On "the tree of life also in the midst of the garden," the reformed Calvin notes the tree of life. Calvin and Gill note that "the tree of life... in the midst of the garden" — the tree of life, set in the garden's center; whether a sacramental sign and pledge of continued life (a sign and seal of the life God gave, to confirm man in life), or a tree whose fruit sustained life; the tree of life was a pledge and means of life, pointing to the life that is in God.

On "the tree of knowledge of good and evil," the grammatical-historical note the test-tree: "the tree of knowledge of good and evil" — the tree by which man's obedience would be tested (v.17); so called because by it man would come to know good and evil experimentally (the good he forsook and the evil he embraced, in the event of disobedience); the tree of the probation, the test of man's loyalty to God. The verse displays the trees of the garden, the tree of life, and the tree of knowledge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the trees — "every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food," the garden's trees beautiful and fruitful, for delight and food (Keil, Ellicott); "the tree of life... in the midst of the garden," the tree of life, a pledge and means of life (Calvin, Gill); "the tree of knowledge of good and evil," the test-tree of man's probation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the garden's trees. The verse marks the trees: pleasant and good. The verse displays the tree of life: in the midst. The verse displays the tree of knowledge: the test. The verse displays the garden: bountiful. The trees display the garden of delight with its two special trees. The verse displays the trees: planted. The verse displays the life: its tree. The verse displays the knowledge: its tree. The trees of the garden, the tree of life, and the tree of knowledge display the furnishing of Eden: God made every tree pleasant and good for food, with the tree of life in the midst and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil — the garden of delight and sustenance, holding the pledge of life and the test of obedience. The verse displays the garden, watered by a river (v.10).
10:
"And a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads." The witnesses note the river of Eden parting into four heads. On "a river went out of Eden to water the garden," the grammatical voices note the watering river. Ellicott and Gill note that "a river went out of Eden to water the garden" — a river flowed from Eden, watering the garden; the river supplied the garden's abundant moisture and fruitfulness; God provided the garden with a copious, living stream.

On "from thence it was parted, and became into four heads," the witnesses note the four rivers. Ellicott and Gill note that "from thence it was parted, and became into four heads" — the river divided into four streams or branches (heads), named in vv.11-14 (Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel, Euphrates); the geography locates Eden in a real region watered by these rivers; the precise identification has been much debated, but the rivers mark Eden as an actual place.

On the river, the reformed Calvin notes the bounty: Calvin and Gill note that the river's abundance and division mark the richness and well-watered fertility of Eden; the garden lacked nothing for its fruitfulness; God's provision is generous and complete; the geography roots the paradise in a real, identifiable land. The verse displays the river of Eden parting into four heads.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the river — "a river went out of Eden to water the garden," the river watering the garden, God's provision of moisture (Ellicott, Gill); "from thence it was parted, and became into four heads," the river dividing into four streams (Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel, Euphrates), locating Eden in a real region (Ellicott, Gill); the river's abundance marking Eden's fertility (Calvin, Gill). The verse displays the river of Eden. The verse marks the river: from Eden. The verse displays the watering: of the garden. The verse displays the parting: four heads. The verse displays the geography: a real region. The river displays Eden well-watered and located by its streams. The verse displays the river: flowing. The verse displays the heads: four. The verse displays the garden: watered. The river of Eden parting into four heads displays the watering of paradise: a river went out of Eden to water the garden and parted into four heads — the abundant stream marking Eden's fertility and locating the garden in a real, watered region. The verse displays the river, the first named Pison (v.11).
11:
"The name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold;" The brief verse notes the first river Pison and the land of Havilah. The grammatical voices note the river. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold" — the first of the four rivers is named Pison, which winds about (compasses) the land of Havilah, a region noted for gold; the geography names the rivers and lands, locating Eden in an actual setting.

On "Pison... which compasseth the whole land of Havilah," the witnesses note the river and land. Keil and Gill note that Pison flows around the land of Havilah; the precise identification of Pison and Havilah is uncertain (variously located), but the river and land are named as real places; the geography marks Eden's region.

On "where there is gold," the grammatical-historical note the gold: "where there is gold" — the land of Havilah is noted for its gold (and other precious things, v.12); the mention of gold and precious stones marks the richness of the lands about Eden; the region is one of natural wealth and beauty. The verse displays the first river Pison and the land of Havilah.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the river — "the name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah," Pison winding about Havilah, a real river and land (Keil, Gill); the identification uncertain but the places real (Keil, Gill); "where there is gold," Havilah noted for its gold and wealth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Pison. The verse marks the river: Pison. The verse displays the land: Havilah. The verse displays the wealth: gold. The verse displays the geography: real. The river displays the first stream and its gold-rich land. The verse displays the Pison: named. The verse displays the Havilah: compassed. The verse displays the gold: present. The first river Pison and the land of Havilah display the geography of Eden's rivers: the first river, Pison, compasses the land of Havilah, where there is gold — the river and land named as real places, marking the richness of Eden's region. The verse displays Havilah's gold and stones (v.12).
12:
"And the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone." The very brief verse notes the precious things of Havilah. The grammatical voices note the wealth of the land. Gill and Clarke note that "the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone" — the land of Havilah (v.11) is rich in good gold, and also in bdellium (an aromatic gum or precious substance) and the onyx stone (a precious gem); the region abounds in natural wealth and precious things; the mention marks the richness and beauty of the lands about Eden.

On "the gold of that land is good," the witnesses note the fine gold. Gill notes that "the gold of that land is good" — the gold of Havilah was of high quality, fine and pure; the land's wealth is noted; the goodness of its gold marks its richness.

On "there is bdellium and the onyx stone," the grammatical-historical note the precious substances: "there is bdellium and the onyx stone" — bdellium (variously identified as a fragrant gum or a precious stone/pearl) and the onyx (a precious gem); the land yields these precious things; the abundance of gold, gum, and gems marks the richness of the region; Eden's setting is one of natural treasure and beauty. The verse displays the precious things of Havilah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wealth — "the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone," Havilah rich in good gold, bdellium, and onyx (Gill, Clarke); "the gold of that land is good," the fine gold of the land (Gill); "there is bdellium and the onyx stone," the precious gum and gem of the region (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rich land. The verse marks the gold: good. The verse displays the bdellium: present. The verse displays the onyx: present. The verse displays the wealth: abundant. The verse displays Havilah's natural treasures. The verse displays the gold: fine. The verse displays the bdellium: precious. The verse displays the onyx: a gem. The precious things of Havilah display the richness of Eden's region: the gold of the land is good, and there is bdellium and the onyx stone — the land abounding in gold, precious gum, and gems, marking the natural wealth and beauty of the country about Eden. The verse displays the rich land, with the second river Gihon (v.13).
13:
"And the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia." The very brief verse notes the second river Gihon and the land of Cush. The grammatical voices note the river. Gill and Ellicott note that "the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia (Cush)" — the second of the four rivers is named Gihon, which winds about the land of Cush (rendered "Ethiopia"); the geography continues to name the rivers and lands of Eden's region.

On "Gihon... that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia," the witnesses note the river and land. Ellicott and JFB note that Gihon flows around the land of Cush; the identification of Gihon and Cush is debated (Cush variously located in Africa or the East); the river and land are named as real places, marking Eden's setting.

On the geography, the grammatical-historical note the real region: the naming of the four rivers (Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel, Euphrates) and their lands locates Eden in an actual, identifiable region; though the precise placement is uncertain (the flood having altered the geography), the rivers mark Eden as a real place, not a myth; the historical, geographical setting of the garden. The verse displays the second river Gihon and the land of Cush.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the river — "the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia," Gihon winding about Cush/Ethiopia (Gill, Ellicott); the identification of Gihon and Cush debated but the places real (Ellicott, JFB); the rivers locating Eden in a real region (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Gihon. The verse marks the river: Gihon. The verse displays the land: Cush. The verse displays the geography: real. The verse displays the region: of Eden. The river displays the second stream and its land. The verse displays the Gihon: named. The verse displays the Cush: compassed. The verse displays the place: real. The second river Gihon and the land of Cush display the continued geography of Eden: the second river, Gihon, compasses the land of Cush — another of the rivers naming Eden's region, marking the garden as a real, located place. The verse displays Gihon, with the third and fourth rivers (v.14).
14:
"And the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates." The brief verse notes the third and fourth rivers, Hiddekel and Euphrates. The grammatical voices note the rivers. Gill and Ellicott note that "the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates" — the third river is Hiddekel (commonly identified with the Tigris), flowing east of Assyria, and the fourth is the Euphrates; the two best-known rivers (the Tigris and Euphrates) anchor the geography of Eden's region.

On "Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria," the witnesses note the Tigris. Ellicott and Gill note that Hiddekel is the Tigris, flowing eastward of Assyria; the well-known river locates Eden's region in the area of Mesopotamia (the land of the Tigris and Euphrates).

On "the fourth river is Euphrates," the grammatical-historical note the Euphrates: "the fourth river is Euphrates" — the Euphrates, the great river, needs no further description (being well known); the naming of the Tigris and Euphrates places Eden's region in or near Mesopotamia, between or about these rivers; the geography roots paradise in the historic land of the great rivers. The verse displays the third and fourth rivers, Hiddekel and Euphrates.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rivers — "the name of the third river is Hiddekel... And the fourth river is Euphrates," the Tigris (Hiddekel) and the Euphrates, the two great rivers (Gill, Ellicott); "Hiddekel... east of Assyria," the Tigris locating Eden's region in Mesopotamia (Ellicott, Gill); "the fourth river is Euphrates," the well-known Euphrates anchoring the geography (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great rivers. The verse marks the third: Hiddekel. The verse displays the fourth: Euphrates. The verse displays the region: Mesopotamia. The verse displays the geography: historic. The rivers display Eden's region anchored by the Tigris and Euphrates. The verse displays the Hiddekel: the Tigris. The verse displays the Euphrates: the great river. The verse displays the land: of the rivers. The third and fourth rivers, Hiddekel and Euphrates, display the anchoring of Eden's geography: the third river Hiddekel (the Tigris) flows east of Assyria, and the fourth is the Euphrates — the two great rivers locating Eden's region in the historic land of Mesopotamia. The verse displays the rivers, watering the garden man is set to keep (v.15).
15:
"And the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it." The witnesses note man placed in the garden to dress and keep it. On "the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden," the grammatical voices note the placing. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden" — God placed man (formed of dust, v.7) in the garden; the man is settled in his appointed dwelling; (the verse resumes and expands v.8, focusing now on man's work and command).

On "to dress it and to keep it," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the appointed work. Henry and Calvin note that man was put in the garden "to dress it (till, cultivate) and to keep it (guard, tend)" — man, even in innocence, was given work: to cultivate and care for the garden; labor is not a result of the fall but a part of man's original, happy estate; idleness was not the condition of paradise; man is to tend and keep God's garden.

On the work, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dignity of labor: man's appointed dressing and keeping of the garden shows that honest labor is good and honorable; God set man to useful work in the place of his innocence; the cultivation of the garden was a happy, fitting employment, not toil or curse (the toil came with the fall, 3:17-19). The verse displays man placed in the garden to dress and keep it.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the placing — "the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden," God settling man in his appointed dwelling (Ellicott, Gill); "to dress it and to keep it," man given the work of cultivating and tending the garden, labor part of innocence (Henry, Calvin); the dignity of honest labor in paradise (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the working man. The verse marks the man: placed. The verse displays the garden: Eden. The verse displays the work: dress and keep. The verse displays the labor: honorable. The placing displays man set to tend God's garden. The verse displays the man: settled. The verse displays the garden: tended. The verse displays the work: appointed. Man placed in the garden to dress and keep it displays man's original employment: the LORD God put the man in the garden of Eden to dress and keep it — man, even in innocence, given the happy, honorable work of cultivating and tending God's garden. The verse displays the working man, given the command of the trees (vv.16-17).
16:
"And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat:" The witnesses note God's gracious permission to eat of every tree. On "the LORD God commanded the man," the grammatical voices note the command. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD God commanded the man" — God gives man a command, the test of his obedience; the command (vv.16-17) is the first law given to man, the trial of his loyalty; God, as man's Lord and benefactor, lays his authority on the man he made.

On "Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the liberality. Henry and Calvin note that "of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat" — God's first word to man is one of generous permission: he may eat freely of every tree (save one, v.17); the bounty and liberality of God toward man; the prohibition is a single, small restraint amid abundant liberty; God's goodness gives much and forbids little.

On the command, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the trial: the command, with its generous permission and single prohibition (v.17), is the test of man's obedience and gratitude; God's liberality leaves no excuse for transgression; the one restraint is the trial of man's loyalty to his Maker; man's happy estate depends on his obedience. The verse displays God's gracious permission to eat of every tree.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the permission — "the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat," God's generous permission to eat of every tree, with one exception (v.17, Keil, Gill); "thou mayest freely eat," the bounty and liberality of God, the prohibition a single small restraint (Henry, Calvin); the command the test of man's obedience and gratitude (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gracious command. The verse marks the command: God's first. The verse displays the permission: every tree. The verse displays the liberty: freely eat. The verse displays the bounty: abundant. The command displays God's generous permission amid the trial. The verse displays the trees: free to eat. The verse displays the liberty: granted. The verse displays the bounty: God's. God's gracious permission to eat of every tree displays the liberality of the first command: the LORD God permitted man to eat freely of every tree of the garden — the generous bounty of God, the single prohibition (v.17) a small restraint amid abundant liberty, the trial of man's obedience. The verse displays the gracious permission, with the one prohibition (v.17).
17:
"But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die." The witnesses note the prohibition and the threatened death. On "of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it," the grammatical voices note the single prohibition. Gill and Clarke note that "of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it" — God forbids man to eat of the one tree (v.9); the single prohibition is the test of man's obedience; the tree is not poisonous in itself, but forbidden by God's command; the trial lies in obedience to God's word.

On "thou shalt not eat of it," the witnesses note the test of obedience. Gill notes that the prohibition is the trial of man's loyalty — whether he will obey God's command and abstain; the one restraint, amid all the liberty (v.16), is the test of man's submission to his Maker; obedience to God's word is the condition of man's happy estate.

On "in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die," the grammatical-historical and witnesses note the threatened death. Gill and Clarke note that "in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die (dying thou shalt die)" — the penalty of disobedience is death; the death threatened embraces spiritual death (separation from God, beginning at once), physical death (the dissolution of body and soul, now made man's lot), and eternal death (the second death, apart from grace); "surely die" (the doubled verb) marks the certainty; in the day of eating, man becomes subject to death. The verse displays the prohibition and the threatened death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prohibition — "of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it," the single forbidden tree, the test of obedience (Gill, Clarke); "thou shalt not eat of it," the trial of man's loyalty to God's word (Gill); "in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die," the threatened death — spiritual, physical, and eternal — the certain penalty of disobedience (Gill, Clarke). The verse displays the prohibition. The verse marks the tree: forbidden. The verse displays the test: obedience. The verse displays the penalty: death. The verse displays the certainty: surely die. The command displays the trial of man and the warning of death. The verse displays the prohibition: given. The verse displays the death: threatened. The verse displays the obedience: required. The prohibition and the threatened death display the test of man: God forbade the tree of knowledge, warning that in the day man ate of it he would surely die — the single prohibition the trial of obedience, and death (spiritual, physical, and eternal) the certain penalty of transgression. The verse displays the tested man, who lacks a helper (v.18).
18:
"And the LORD God said, It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him." The witnesses note God's purpose to make a helper for man. On "It is not good that the man should be alone," the grammatical voices note the divine observation. Ellicott and Gill note that "It is not good that the man should be alone" — God declares man's solitary state not good; man, made for society and companionship, was incomplete alone; the only thing in the creation pronounced "not good" is man's solitude; God purposes to remedy it; man needs a companion suited to him.

On "It is not good that the man should be alone," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the need. Henry and Calvin note that man's being alone was not good — he needed a companion and helper; God, who knows man's nature, provides for his need; marriage and human society are God's gracious provision; man is made for fellowship.

On "I will make him an help meet for him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the helper: "I will make him an help meet (a help suitable, corresponding) for him" — God purposes to make a helper fit and corresponding to man; the woman, made to be his suitable partner, helper, and companion; "meet for him" (suitable, answering to him) marks her fitness as his counterpart; the divine institution of the woman as man's helper and the foundation of marriage. The verse displays God's purpose to make a helper for man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the need — "It is not good that the man should be alone," man's solitude not good, man made for companionship (Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin); "I will make him an help meet for him," God purposing a suitable helper, the woman as man's fit companion (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the purposed helper. The verse marks the solitude: not good. The verse displays the need: companionship. The verse displays the helper: meet for him. The verse displays the institution: of marriage. The purpose displays God providing man a suitable companion. The verse displays the man: alone. The verse displays the helper: purposed. The verse displays the woman: fit for him. God's purpose to make a helper for man displays the provision of companionship: God said it was not good for man to be alone and purposed to make him a helper suitable for him — man made for fellowship, the woman God's gracious provision as his fit companion and helper, the foundation of marriage. The verse displays the purposed helper, sought first among the animals (vv.19-20).
19:
"And out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof." The witnesses note the animals brought to Adam to be named. On "out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air," the grammatical voices note the creatures. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air" — the animals, made of the ground (as their bodies, ch. 1), are here brought before man; (the verse does not assert a new creation but recalls the creatures' origin and their being brought to Adam); the beasts and birds are presented to man.

On "brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them," the reformed Calvin notes the naming. Calvin and Gill note that God "brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them" — God presents the creatures to Adam to name them; the naming shows Adam's authority and dominion over the creatures (1:28), and his knowledge and wisdom in discerning their natures; man, as lord of the creatures, gives them their names.

On "whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof," the grammatical-historical note Adam's wisdom: "whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof" — Adam's names stood; his naming reflected the natures of the creatures (his original wisdom and insight); and the naming, while displaying man's dominion, also showed that among all the creatures none was a fit helper for man (v.20) — preparing for the making of the woman. The verse displays the animals brought to Adam to be named.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creatures — "out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air," the animals presented to man (Keil, Ellicott); "brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them," God presenting the creatures for Adam to name, showing his dominion and wisdom (Calvin, Gill); "whatsoever Adam called... that was the name thereof," Adam's names standing, his wisdom displayed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming. The verse marks the animals: brought. The verse displays the naming: by Adam. The verse displays the dominion: man's. The verse displays the wisdom: Adam's. The act displays Adam naming the creatures in his dominion. The verse displays the creatures: presented. The verse displays the names: given. The verse displays the man: lord. The animals brought to Adam to be named display man's dominion and wisdom: God formed the beasts and birds and brought them to Adam to name, and his names stood — Adam, as lord of the creatures, naming them in his original wisdom, the naming also showing none was a fit helper for him. The verse displays the named creatures, none meet for Adam (v.20).
20:
"And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him." The brief verse notes Adam naming the creatures and finding no fit helper. The grammatical voices note the naming and the lack. Ellicott and Gill note that "Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him" — Adam named all the creatures (v.19), but among them all there was found no helper suitable for him; the naming displayed his dominion, but also made evident his solitude; none of the animals could be his companion and counterpart.

On "Adam gave names to all cattle... and to every beast of the field," the witnesses note the completed naming. Ellicott and Gill note that Adam named all the classes of creatures (cattle, fowl, beasts); the naming was comprehensive; man exercised his dominion over all the living creatures.

On "but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him," the grammatical-historical note the want: "but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him" — among all the creatures, none was a fit helper, companion, or counterpart for man; the beasts, though under his dominion, could not supply his need for fellowship; the lack prepares for and underscores the gift of the woman (vv.21-22); man needed one of his own nature. The verse displays Adam naming the creatures and finding no fit helper.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the naming and lack — "Adam gave names to all cattle... but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him," Adam naming all the creatures, yet finding no fit helper among them (Ellicott, Gill); the comprehensive naming displaying his dominion (Ellicott, Gill); "there was not found an help meet for him," none of the creatures a fit companion, preparing for the woman (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unmet need. The verse marks the naming: all creatures. The verse displays the dominion: man's. The verse displays the lack: no fit helper. The verse displays the preparation: for the woman. The naming displays man's dominion and his unmet need for a companion. The verse displays the creatures: named. The verse displays the helper: not found. The verse displays the need: unmet. Adam naming the creatures and finding no fit helper displays man's dominion and solitude: Adam named all the creatures, but among them found no helper meet for him — his dominion over the creatures evident, yet none of them his fit companion, the lack preparing for the gift of the woman. The verse displays the unmet need, supplied by the woman (vv.21-22).
21:
"And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof;" The witnesses note the deep sleep and the taking of the rib. On "the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam," the grammatical voices note the sleep. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD God caused a deep sleep (tardemah) to fall upon Adam" — God put Adam into a deep, supernatural sleep, that he might feel no pain and witness nothing of the operation; the deep sleep is God's gracious provision while he forms the woman from him; man's part is passive, the work wholly God's.

On "he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rib. Calvin and Gill note that God "took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof" — God took a rib (or a part of Adam's side) and closed the flesh; the woman is made from man's own body, from his side; the taking of the rib (not from the head to rule him, nor the foot to be trampled, but the side to be his equal and near his heart, as the expositors observe) marks the woman's intimate union with man.

On the making of woman from man, the puritan Henry notes the significance: Henry notes that the woman, made from man's rib, is bone of his bone (v.23); the manner of her making teaches the closeness, union, and mutual love of husband and wife; she is taken from his side, to be his companion and equal, dear to him as his own flesh. The verse displays the deep sleep and the taking of the rib.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sleep — "the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam," God putting Adam into a deep sleep for the painless forming of the woman (Ellicott, Gill); "he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh," God taking a rib from Adam's side to make the woman (Calvin, Gill); the woman made from man's side, the sign of their union and equality (Henry). The verse displays the taken rib. The verse marks the sleep: deep. The verse displays the rib: taken. The verse displays the flesh: closed. The verse displays the woman: from man's side. The act displays God forming the woman from man's body. The verse displays the sleep: divine. The verse displays the rib: from his side. The verse displays the union: signified. The deep sleep and the taking of the rib display the making of the woman: God caused a deep sleep to fall on Adam and took a rib from his side — the woman formed from man's own body, the manner of her making teaching the close union, equality, and love of husband and wife. The verse displays the taken rib, made into the woman (v.22).
22:
"And the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman, and brought her unto the man." The brief verse notes the making of the woman and her presentation to man. The grammatical voices note the building of the woman. Ellicott and Gill note that "the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made (built, banah) he a woman, and brought her unto the man" — God formed (the word means "built") the woman from the rib taken from man; the woman is God's own handiwork, built from man's side; God then brings her to the man, presenting her as his helper and bride.

On "made he a woman," the reformed Calvin notes the building. Calvin and Gill note that God "built" the woman from the rib — the word marks the careful, skillful forming of the woman; she is fashioned by God from man's body; the woman is no afterthought but God's deliberate, gracious provision, built to be man's counterpart.

On "brought her unto the man," the grammatical-historical note the presentation: "brought her unto the man" — God himself brings the woman to Adam, presenting her as his helper and wife; God is, as it were, the author and witness of the first marriage; the woman, God's gift, is given to the man; the divine institution of marriage, God joining the man and woman. The verse displays the making of the woman and her presentation to man.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the building — "the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman," God building the woman from man's rib, his own handiwork (Ellicott, Gill); "made (built) he a woman," the careful forming of the woman as man's counterpart (Calvin, Gill); "brought her unto the man," God presenting the woman to Adam, the first marriage (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the made woman. The verse marks the rib: made a woman. The verse displays the building: God's. The verse displays the presentation: to the man. The verse displays the marriage: instituted. The act displays God building the woman and giving her to man. The verse displays the woman: made. The verse displays the man: given her. The verse displays the union: begun. The making of the woman and her presentation to man display the gift of the helper: God built the woman from the rib and brought her to the man — the woman God's own handiwork, given to Adam as his helper and bride, God himself the author of the first marriage. The verse displays the given woman, received with joy (v.23).
23:
"And Adam said, This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man." The witnesses note Adam's recognition of the woman as his own flesh. On "This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh," the grammatical voices note Adam's joyful recognition. Ellicott and Gill note that "This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh" — Adam, awaking, recognizes the woman as part of himself, taken from his own body; "now at last" (this is now) marks his glad recognition, after the creatures had yielded no fit helper (v.20); the woman is of his own nature, bone and flesh of his.

On "bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the union. Calvin and Clarke note that the woman, being "bone of his bones and flesh of his flesh," is of one nature and substance with man; the closest union and kinship; husband and wife are one flesh (v.24); the woman is man's equal and intimate companion, not of a different or inferior kind.

On "she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man," the grammatical-historical note the naming: "she shall be called Woman (ishshah), because she was taken out of Man (ish)" — Adam names her "Woman" (ishshah), corresponding to "Man" (ish), the name marking her origin from man and her kinship with him; the wordplay (ish/ishshah) expresses their unity and correspondence; the woman is named by the man, as one of his own. The verse displays Adam's recognition of the woman as his own flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the recognition — "This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh," Adam's glad recognition of the woman as part of himself (Ellicott, Gill); "bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh," the woman of one nature with man, the closest union (Calvin, Clarke); "she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man," the naming marking her origin and kinship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recognized woman. The verse marks the recognition: bone of my bones. The verse displays the union: flesh of my flesh. The verse displays the name: Woman. The verse displays the origin: from Man. The recognition displays Adam owning the woman as his own flesh. The verse displays the woman: recognized. The verse displays the union: of nature. The verse displays the name: ishshah. Adam's recognition of the woman as his own flesh displays the joy of the first union: Adam recognized the woman as bone of his bone and flesh of his flesh, and named her Woman, taken out of Man — the woman of his own nature, his fit companion and equal, the closest kinship of husband and wife. The verse displays the recognized woman, the foundation of marriage (v.24).
24:
"Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh." The witnesses note the institution of marriage and one flesh. On "Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife," the grammatical voices note the marriage ordinance. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife" — the verse draws from the making of the woman (vv.21-23) the ordinance of marriage: a man shall leave his parents and cleave (be joined, united) to his wife; marriage is the closest of human bonds, taking precedence even over the tie to father and mother; the man and wife form a new union.

On "shall cleave unto his wife," the reformed Calvin and puritan note the union. Calvin and Gill note that the man is to "cleave (be firmly joined, hold fast) unto his wife" — the marriage bond is permanent, faithful, and exclusive; the husband cleaves to his wife in a lasting union; marriage is a lifelong, faithful cleaving, not to be broken (cf. Matthew 19:5-6, where Christ cites this verse for the permanence of marriage).

On "they shall be one flesh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the one flesh: "they shall be one flesh" — the man and wife become one flesh, a single union; the deepest bodily, personal, and spiritual union; the two become one; the foundation of marriage as instituted by God at creation, before the fall; Christ and Paul build on this (Matthew 19; Ephesians 5:31) for the doctrine of marriage. The verse displays the institution of marriage and one flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ordinance — "a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife," the marriage bond, taking precedence over the parental tie (Keil, Ellicott); "shall cleave unto his wife," the permanent, faithful, exclusive union (Calvin, Gill; Matthew 19:5-6); "they shall be one flesh," the man and wife one union, the deepest bond (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays marriage instituted. The verse marks the leaving: father and mother. The verse displays the cleaving: to the wife. The verse displays the union: one flesh. The verse displays the institution: of marriage. The ordinance displays God's institution of marriage as one flesh. The verse displays the man: cleaving. The verse displays the wife: joined. The verse displays the flesh: one. The institution of marriage and one flesh displays the foundation of wedlock: a man shall leave his parents and cleave to his wife, and they shall be one flesh — marriage instituted by God at creation, the closest, permanent, faithful union of husband and wife, the two made one. The verse displays the one-flesh union, of the unashamed pair (v.25).
25:
"And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed." The witnesses note the innocence of the unashamed pair. On "they were both naked, the man and his wife," the grammatical voices note the original state. Ellicott and Gill note that "they were both naked, the man and his wife" — Adam and Eve were naked, without clothing; in their original innocence, they had no need of covering; the unfallen pair were unclothed yet undisturbed.

On "and were not ashamed," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the innocence. Calvin and Gill note that they "were not ashamed" — their nakedness brought no shame, for there was as yet no sin, no lust, no consciousness of evil; their innocence was perfect; shame entered only with sin (3:7, when their eyes were opened); the absence of shame marks the purity and integrity of the unfallen state.

On the innocence, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the contrast with the fall: the pair's nakedness without shame marks their original righteousness and innocence; after the fall (3:7), they knew shame and sought to cover themselves; the verse closes the account of the creation in the happy, innocent state of man and wife before sin entered; the loss of this innocence is the tragedy of the fall (ch. 3). The verse displays the innocence of the unashamed pair.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the innocence — "they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed," the unfallen pair naked yet unashamed (Ellicott, Gill); "and were not ashamed," their innocence perfect, no sin or shame (Calvin, Gill); the nakedness without shame marking original righteousness, lost in the fall (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the innocent pair. The verse marks the pair: naked. The verse displays the state: unashamed. The verse displays the innocence: perfect. The verse displays the contrast: with the fall. The state displays man and wife innocent before sin. The verse displays the nakedness: without shame. The verse displays the innocence: original. The verse displays the purity: unbroken. The innocence of the unashamed pair displays the close of the creation account: the man and his wife were both naked and not ashamed — their original innocence and righteousness perfect, without sin or shame, the happy state of man before the fall. The verse closes the account of paradise, before the entrance of the serpent (ch.3).
